title: Spectral Politics
abstract:
Abstract
               This final chapter examines how political powers have sought to capture the power of spectres. It is first of all about its capacity to represent an absent power: as a spiritual body, the king is in a sense always a ghost ‘already there’, capable of appearing everywhere and at any time. It also concerns the legitimising power of ghosts: the ghost is the bearer of a prophetic, extra-lucid word, in contact with the divinity as well as with the deep genealogies of royalty. But the king does not have a monopoly on the manipulation of ghosts: there are several of them in the polemics of the sixteenth century, to the point that in the middle of the seventeenth century, the king sought to exorcise ghosts more than to enlist them in his service. This power of exorcism met with other changes, notably the end of the Wars of Religion, the rise of experimental science, and the affirmation of a scholarly decorum that kept the preternatural at a distance. The ‘spectral moment’ of the long Renaissance was coming to an end, in the expectation of a future ‘recharge’.
